claim: OpenAI's GPT-OSS models are open-weight (not open-source) with Apache 2.0 license, meaning you get weights but not training code, data, or checkpoints
actionable: Understand the distinction: open-weight lets you run and modify models locally, but open-source (like AI2 models) includes training reproducibility

claim: GPT-OSS 120B (117B active) and 20B (3.6B active) are mixture-of-experts models, compared to proprietary o3/o4 models with higher active parameter ratios
actionable: When evaluating these models, note the active parameter count (what actually runs) vs total size; lower active parameters means faster inference but may affect quality

claim: GPT-OSS models support three reasoning effort levels (low/medium/high) that trade off latency for performance, set via system prompt
actionable: Experiment with reasoning effort settings; high effort uses roughly 80% of max_tokens for reasoning, adjust based on latency vs quality needs

claim: GPT-OSS models have a knowledge cutoff around June 2024, making them outdated for current events; they incorrectly list Biden as current US president in 2025
actionable: Don't use GPT-OSS for real-time information or current events; pair with retrieval or web search tools if temporal accuracy matters

claim: Running GPT-OSS 20B locally requires Triton installed for 4-bit quantization; without it, models load in 16-bit and become too large for typical hardware
actionable: To run GPT-OSS 20B with Ollama or transformers, ensure Triton is installed; without it, expect the model to be too large for most systems

claim: GPT-OSS models perform well on function calling and agent benchmarks, with the 20B model scoring comparably to o3 Mini on some benchmarks
actionable: Consider GPT-OSS 20B for local agent implementations; it's competitive with proprietary mini models for tool use and function calling
